node_2: multiclass confidences and bounding box localization
edge: The proposed train-time calibration method aims to jointly calibrate multiclass confidences and bounding box localization for improved object detection performance.
The core of the proposed train-time calibration method is an auxiliary loss term that aids in calibrating multiclass confidences and bounding box localization.
The auxiliary loss term leverages predictive uncertainty in multiclass confidences and bounding box localization for improved calibration.
The auxiliary loss term is differentiable and operates on minibatches, which can be utilized with other task-specific loss functions.

node_2: calibrating multiclass confidence and bounding box localization
edge: The train-time calibration method aims at calibrating both multiclass confidence and bounding box localization for improved object detection.
Calibration in classification ensures that model outputs match the predictive accuracy, preventing overconfidence or underconfidence.
A model is perfectly calibrated when the predicted confidences match the actual accuracy for each sample in the dataset.
In (image) classification, a model's class confidences should align with the actual accuracy to be considered calibrated.
Each input example contributes to the calibration of the model by evaluating predicted confidences against ground truth labels.
Calibration in object detection serves a different purpose compared to classification, emphasizing the need for calibration techniques specific to object localization.

Object detection and classification are two types of tasks in the field of computer vision. While classification focuses on assigning a single label to an entire image, object detection involves localizing and classifying multiple objects within an image.
In object detection, the dataset contains ground-truth annotations for each object in an image, including localization information and associated object categories.
In object detection, object localization is represented by a bounding box annotation, denoted as b* in the range of [0,1]^4.
The bounding box annotation in object detection is associated with specific object categories present in the image.
An object detection model predicts a class label (^y) along with a confidence score (^s) for a detected object.
In object detection, precision is used for calibration instead of accuracy. Precision focuses on the proportion of correctly predicted object detections to all predicted object detections.
In object detection, calibration is achieved not by focusing on accuracy but by ensuring precision in predictions.
A perfectly calibrated object detector accurately predicts class labels (^y) with confidence scores (^s) and bounding boxes (^b) that match ground-truth annotations.
The expected calibration error (ECE) is utilized to measure the miscalibration of a model in both classification and object detection tasks.
In calibration, confidence is used to estimate the predictive accuracy of a model in making correct predictions.
ECE is approximated by binning the confidence space into N equally spaced bins.
ECE can be used for measuring miscalibration of object detectors.
ECE fails to reflect calibration improvement when additional box coordinates are used for calibration.
ECE considers confidence of each example independent of the box properties to apply binning and to calculate an average precision.
In this work, we use location-dependent calibration, termed as detection ECE (D-ECE).
D-ECE is defined as the expected deviation of the observed precision with respect to the given box properties.
Similar to ECE, the multidimensional D-ECE is calculated by partitioning both the confidence and box property spaces into Nk equally spaced bins.

In multi-class confidence calibration, class-wise certainty is fused with mean confidence to create the multiclass fusion of mean confidence and certainty.
